title: £100,000 of TikTok sales in a day: The business of live shopping
description: Daisy's business goes live from a studio for a minimum of six hours a day with different presenters interacting with shoppers, from showing off produc
body: Daisy's business goes live from a studio for a minimum of six hours a day with different presenters interacting with shoppers, from showing off products to answering their questions."It's like having 500 people in your shop," she says.There are a host of platforms and marketplaces now offering livestreams connecting sellers and customers, from Instagram Live, YouTube Shopping, to eBay and Amazon Live. They're all different.Live shopping, it seems, is becoming part of everyday consumer behaviour for many.According to new research from retail agency Savvy Marketing, 30% of shoppers surveyed said they'd bought something from a live shopping event."The big retailers have got to grab hold of this," says Catherine Shuttleworth, CEO of Savvy. "It's grown from nothing to a huge thing really quickly. If you haven't got a strategy for live selling, you're going to miss out."TikTok Shop says its UK sales grew by more than 30% year-on-year in June, with live shopping the fastest-growing format.It says it now has more than 6,000 livestreams taking place every day.At an event in the centuries old Covent Garden market, it hosted 20 small businesses with stalls who were also selling live to customers all over the UK.With nothing more than a smartphone, a lighting kit and a fridge full of fresh meat, halal butcher Manny Malik from Wolverhampton had around two hundred people tuning into his session."Last week we did a TikTok live for three hours and we sold £8,000 worth of meat," he says.It would normally take him three to four days to sell this amount in his family shop, Malik Butchers.They're now opening a second store in Stoke. TikTok, he says, is helping to drive his expansion.But there are fees.TikTok Shop UK keeps up to 9p from every £1 sold on the platform, as its standard commission. As a comparison, eBay's marketplace business sellers typically pay between 6.9p and 14.9p depending on the category. Businesses selling on the dedicated live selling format, eBay Live, pay a combined fee of up to 8.42p including payment processing."It's a lot," says Manny."For us in the meat game, there aren't huge margins. However, it's opened up a new platform. We didn't have these sales prior to TikTok. We're not losing money, we've got enough profitability in there," he adds.Buket Sevik, 34, founded her jewellery brand Adorina in February after quitting her 9-5 job as a business manager. Her new business went from 50 orders a month to 500 orders a week since she started live selling on TikTok UK shop in May. She's gone from working from her dining table to live streaming from a small unit, where orders are now being packed."It's crazy," she laughs.Demand is so high, she's trying to hire someone to help.But for Buket, the best thing about livestreaming is the flexibility. "I can go live whenever I want... which means it works absolutely perfectly for me as a mum. I can put my daughter to sleep and I can just hit go live and do some sales."Savvy's latest survey of shoppers also found that 66% of Gen Y and Gen Z shoppers said seeing products sell out or go viral made them more likely to buy.That's something that worries the free debt advice service, Money Wellness, which warns live shopping events are "a recipe for overspending", says external relations manager Rebecca Lamb."Every time you snap up a bargain before it sells out, your brain gets a dopamine hit - the same rush you get from gambling," she says."And because a lot offer one tap payment, there's no card, no basket and no total. Often you've spent money before your brain has caught up," she says.

schema.headline: £100,000 of TikTok sales in a day: The business of live shopping

title: UK government borrowing lower than expected in June
description: The government borrowed slightly less than expected in June, according to official figures published as new prime minister Andy Burnham began setting
body: The government borrowed slightly less than expected in June, according to official figures published as new prime minister Andy Burnham began setting out measures to cut living costs for households.  Borrowing - the difference between spending and income from taxes - was £16bn, £7.9bn lower a year earlier, although analysts said challenges remained over the UK's public finances.Separate data showed the unemployment rate was unchanged, with the Office for National Statistics (ONS) saying the labour market was "relatively steady".However, while the borrowing figure was less than forecast, the ONS said total debt remained near £3 trillion - close to the annual value of the entire UK economy.Borrowing for June was slightly below the £16.3bn that had been predicted by the government's official forecaster, the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR).Ruth Gregory, deputy chief UK economist at Capital Economics, said June's figure was "a rare piece of good news" for the new prime minister and his new Chancellor, John Healey.However, she added: "Overall, there's no escaping the fact that the public finances are fragile and that there is limited scope for extra borrowing."So far in the current financial year, borrowing has reached a total of £57.6bn, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS). While this is down £3.7bn from the same period last year, it is £2.7bn above the OBR's forecast.James Smith, chief UK economist at ING, told the BBC's Today programme the fact that borrowing was still running ahead of the OBR's projections was "a reminder of the challenges that the new chancellor and the new prime minister face".He added they would face a "difficult picture" at the autumn Budget, with "lots of tough choices to be made".Burnham and Healey have both pledged to stick to former chancellor Rachel Reeves' fiscal rules on spending and borrowing. although the new prime minister said on Monday he would use "any flexibility within them" to help with policy changes. Shortly after Burnham's comments were made public, the yield on 10-year government bonds - effectively the interest rate charged to the UK government for a 10-year loan - rose above 5%.The yield hit 5.03% late on Monday afternoon. However, when trading began on Tuesday the rate slipped back slightly to 5.01%.In a statement released on Monday, Healey said "fiscal credibility is the bedrock for economic stability and for national security".The new leadership's first major policy announcement was to cut VAT on household electricity bills from 5% to zero from the beginning of October.Ministers said it would be funded by savings from the cancellation of the digital ID programme, but Labour's Darren Jones, who was sacked as chief secretary to the prime minister on Monday, accused the government of announcing an unfunded tax cut.June's borrowing figure was helped by higher revenues from income tax and VAT, while interest payments on inflation-linked debt fell.The government paid £11.8bn in debt interest payments in June, which was nearly a third lower than the same point last year. However, it was still the fourth highest total for June on record, the ONS said.Meanwhile, the latest survey of the labour market showed the unemployment rate remained unchanged at 4.9%.Growth in regular earnings - which excludes bonuses - remained unchanged, rising at an annual pace of 3.4% in the March to May period.However, the ONS noted that regular wage growth in the private sector fell below 3% for the first time since 2020.Yael Selfin, chief economist at KPMG, said the "subdued" wage growth made it more likely that the Bank of England would keep interest rates on hold at 3.75% when it meets next week."Weak hiring activity is continuing to weigh on workers' bargaining power, limiting upward pressure on wages," she added."Workers are also set to see a renewed squeeze on living standards during the second half of the year as higher energy costs feed through to household bills."

schema.headline: UK government borrowing lower than expected in June

title: Trump slaps 50% tariffs on Canada and Carney vows to 'intensify' trade talks
description: US President Donald Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, in retaliation for what he called "unequal treatment
body: US President Donald Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, in retaliation for what he called "unequal treatment" of US cars, dairy and alcohol. Everyday consumer items like wine and hockey sticks and industrial goods such as cement are among the goods targeted. However, several key exports will be spared, such as energy, potash, critical minerals and fish. Prime Minister Mark Carney responded by saying Canada stood ready to "intensify" trade talks with the US in the coming weeks.The White House said the duties would take effect in 30 days. It marks a major escalation in trade tensions between the North American neighbours. These tensions have been simmering since Trump returned to office in January 2025 and unleashed a wide-ranging global programme of tariffs. Tariffs are taxes on imported goods that are paid to the government by companies bringing in the foreign products.The US Supreme Court ruled earlier this year that many of Trump's tariffs imposed globally under emergency powers were illegally enacted. But his latest action on Monday night uses a different, obscure law that is untested in court.Canada, which is one of the US's closest trading partners, was one of the few countries to retaliate last year against Trump's tariffs. It placed a 25% levy of its own on about C$30bn (£16bn; $21.7bn) worth of US goods being brought into Canada. Carney later dropped some of them.A White House fact sheet published on Monday discussing the new tariffs said they applied regardless of whether the product was included under the existing free trade agreement between Canada, the US and Mexico, known as the USMCA.The new tariffs add to trade barriers already in place between the two nations. The US has been maintaining active tariffs ranging from 15% to 50% on Canadian steel, aluminium and copper. It also charges a 35% tariff on Canadian softwood lumber, alongside a 25% tax on non-US parts in cars. Meanwhile Canada has its own 25% counter-tariff on selected imports of American steel, aluminium and vehicles. Monday's announcement also comes in the wake of President Trump's threat to impose tariffs over Canadian wildfire smoke drifting into US cities, though this issue has not been officially mentioned as the justification for the move.As part of his executive action announcing the new tariffs, Trump issued three proclamations.These list US complaints over trade issues that were previously known to Canada  - signalling a breakdown of trade negotiations between the two neighbours. They relate to cars, dairy products and alcohol.On cars, Trump's proclamation pointed to Canada charging a tax on imports of US motor vehicles and parts that are not covered under USMCA.He argues it is "unreasonable" and that Canada has discriminated against the US by not charging other countries a similar tax.Automotive manufacturing in North America is highly integrated between Canada, the US and Mexico.Dairy, meanwhile, has long been a problem for the US, specifically Canada's supply management system, which sets limits on foreign imports. Those that exceed the limit are charged a tariff up to 300%.And lastly, the enduring boycott of US alcoholic drinks by most Canadian provinces has become a major sore point for the Americans since it was imposed last year. Canadian premiers have said repeatedly that the boycott will be lifted if the US removes its tariffs on key Canadian sectors, including metals and automobiles.Canadian trade negotiators have been trying to secure a deal that would at least reduce some of the current US tariffs.In February, the US Supreme Court struck down sweeping international tariffs imposed by Trump through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) of 1977. Trump had used IEEPA to attempt to justify the imposition of tariffs on dozens of countries beyond America's neighbours.The Supreme Court justices ruled that the president had exceeded his authority when he announced the duties under a law reserved for national emergencies. The White House vowed at the time that it would invoke other mechanisms to impose import taxes. The tariffs announced on Monday that target Canada specifically were introduced under Section 338 of the 1930 Tariff Act, which covers trade discrimination rather than national emergencies. Michael Devereux, a professor of economics at the University of British Columbia, said the move highlighted the Trump administration's opposition to USMCA. He told BBC News: "It is a significant escalation because it directly targets goods that were previously exempt under the US, Canada, Mexico trade agreement that President Trump negotiated and signed himself in 2018."Devereux was doubtful that it was a negotiating tactic on the part of the US leader. "I would rather see this as just an impulsive move that came from kind of a grudge that the US government and President Trump has against Canada."Other analysts were hopeful the move might prompt new negotiations.Candance Laing, head of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, urged officials to make "meaningful progress" in talks before the new duties took effect in 30 days' time.Chris Swonger, head of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, similarly called for both sides to find a solution, warning the decision "raises the risk of further retaliation".

schema.headline: Trump slaps 50% tariffs on Canada and Carney vows to 'intensify' trade talks

title: Counterfeit football kits worth £18m seized as Fire Service warns of fire risk
description: Counterfeit football shirts worth over £18m were seized in the UK during the course of the World Cup because of safety concerns. The tournament produc
body: Counterfeit football shirts worth over £18m were seized in the UK during the course of the World Cup because of safety concerns. The tournament produced a spike in the sales of fake shirts and kits, but the Fire Service has warned that these items are far more flammable and dangerous than the genuine articles.More than 220,000 pieces of football merchandise were recovered by the Police Intellectual Crime Unit as part of Operation Bloxwich, a UK police enforcement effort targeting counterfeit goods.One raid involved 1,618 fake strips being taken from a warehouse in Birmingham, including replicas of star players such as France's Kylian Mbappe and Norway's Erling Haaland.Detective Sergeant Jamie Kirk, City of London Police, who took part in the operation, told BBC Sport: "I understand why people want to buy these shirts, people love football and the incentive is the lower price - but these shirts, they're not regulated and could be highly flammable."They have dyes in them which could be harmful to skin, particularly children's skin.   While the cost is lower - and that's the attractive part - potential damages to you and the people making them is just far too high."Cheshire Fire and Rescue Service tested the flammability of counterfeit shirts compared with genuine kit and found the fake kits were much more flammable."It's quite clear that the counterfeit one melted as soon as the burner went on it," said watch manager Dave Jackson, who carried out the test by hanging them two at a time on an iron bar and used blow torches to set them alight. "I really had to try to make the genuine one ignite and melt."It's extremely dangerous. It's something that can cause horrific injuries to your children. It's just not worth the savings."Kate Caffrey, head of intelligence at the Intellectual Property Office, warned that fake shirts are not regulated."Counterfeit strips are not subject to health and safety regulations, British Standards [or] EU standards," she told BBC Sport."We've got evidence that counterfeit goods are made in appalling conditions, so you just don't know what's in them, how they're being made and there's no way of providing any safety reassurances."Counterfeiting is criminal, these are manufactured and supplied by criminal networks, criminal gangs and they're very adept at targeting markets where there's demand. So the World Cup, the forthcoming Premier League season - they're going to flood the market with fake shirts."The price of a fake shirt, you don't know what you're funding with that money."

schema.headline: Counterfeit football kits worth £18m seized as Fire Service warns of fire risk

title: Will the Tate brothers be extradited from the US to the UK?
description: The CPS said on Sunday that Andrew was being charged with seven further counts of rape, three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexu
body: The CPS said on Sunday that Andrew was being charged with seven further counts of rape, three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual exploitation, three counts of assault occasioning actual bodily harm and 19 additional charges for offences relating to indecent images of a child and extreme pornography.Tristan's new charges include two counts of rape, one count of sexual assault and three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual exploitation.The alleged offending is said to have taken place between July 2010 and August 2017.Andrew now faces a total of 42 charges in the UK and Tristan faces 17 charges, after CPS had pursued other charges last year."These charging decisions followed receipt of a further file of evidence from Bedfordshire Police and bring the total number of alleged victims in this case to seven," Malcolm McHaffie, Head of the Special Crime Division at the CPS, said.The brothers have been based in Romania, where they are also under criminal investigation. In 2024, Bedfordshire Police secured European arrest warrants for the pair to have them returned from Romania when proceedings against them there conclude. They deny all allegations against them. The brothers have been able to travel to the US since travel restrictions against them in Romania were lifted in early 2025.A US Department of Justice spokesperson said the arrests were "pursuant to extradition proceedings".The UK and the US are parties to a treaty that allows the extradition of people in relation to alleged offences punishable under laws in both countries by at least one year in prison.For a UK case, the CPS prepares a request which is certified by a judge. The final request is sent to be considered by the Home Office, which then forwards the extradition request to the relevant state through usual diplomatic channels.The decision to accept the extradition request is taken by the US State Department. The request will then be considered by the US courts, with a final decision made by US Secretary of State Marco Rubio.Appearing in a federal courtroom in Miami on Monday, the pair wore light brown prison uniforms and were handcuffed and shackled at the waist and ankles.They spoke only when asked if they understood their rights to which they replied together "yes your honour".The brothers' attorney, Joe McBride, told reporters after the brief hearing that his clients are innocent."They've never done nothing wrong. They shouldn't be extradited for crimes they didn't commit," McBride said."It's garbage. It's a stitch up job," he added.He compared the charges against the men to the legal proceedings against US President Donald Trump - which he called a "witch hunt" - and referred to the attempted extradition as "political kidnapping".When asked how long the extradition proceedings would take, McBride estimated ahead of the hearing that it would be "around 60 days".Durham Law School Associate Professor Gemma Davies said there was a clause in the US-UK treaty that prevents extradition for political offences, adding lawyers for the Tates could argue the allegations are politically motivated."However, it's very rare extradition between the UK and US is refused on those grounds," Davies said, adding the test would be subject to a high threshold.She said it was "relatively rare" for an extradition request between the two countries to be refused given the strong working relationship between authorities."It's fairly common to extradite between the two, which means it's well traversed from a legal perspective, and we are two countries that have fairly similar legal systems in terms of criminal justice," Davies said.But Bill Bowring, Emeritus Professor of Law at Birkbeck College, University of London, said the ongoing proceedings in Romania were a "major complication".He said he had not "seen a satisfactory answer" as to why the CPS was now pursuing extradition from the US after a Romanian court ruled in 2024 the pair could only be extradited from that country after the conclusion of their separate case there.The BBC has requested comment from the CPS in relation to its position on the Romanian proceedings.Davies said that if the CPS was successful, the extradition could still take months, with any appeal delaying the process by about a year, "if not more".

schema.headline: Will the Tate brothers be extradited from the US to the UK?

title: Starmer ally takes swipe at Burnham's first big policy
description: Whenever there is a reshuffle at the top of government, it is worth keeping an eye and an ear on the dislodged, demoted and rejected former ministers.
body: Whenever there is a reshuffle at the top of government, it is worth keeping an eye and an ear on the dislodged, demoted and rejected former ministers. What do they choose to do and say next?Andy Burnham has started his first full day in the job with a promise to cut VAT from household electricity bills from 1 October.But already – within hours - he is taking flak from a man who just yesterday was in a senior job in Downing Street working alongside Sir Keir Starmer.On Monday night, the former Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister, Darren Jones, posted a picture on X of him having a beer, external, captioned "thank you and good night. See you September".If that was meant to suggest a vow of silence it only lasted a little over 12 hours, because at breakfast time this morning came another post, this one unaccompanied by any picture but loaded with political bite."Good news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills," wrote Jones. "It's a simple way for families to save a few quid, and to mechanically help to keep inflation that little bit lower. But the DigitalID program was unfunded. The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget."Ouch - that will sting in Downing Street.And yes, there are four references in the Office for Budget Responsibility's Economic and Fiscal Outlook from November, external of last year that refer to Digital ID and how and where the money will come from to pay for it is not clear.For example, on page 10, it says "the cost of digital ID cards, for which no specific funding has been identified". On Page 172 it talks about "the risks around the implementation of digital ID cards for which no explicit provision has been made for its £0.6bn annual cost".Clearly, this was something of a critique of the funding of the policy Jones was previously an advocate of, so the charge was as relevant to him and Sir Keir Starmer's government about their idea as it is to Andy Burnham and his new idea.But it is Burnham who is now prime minister and has to set out, as he put it in his first speech in Downing Street, "how we pay for" the policies they are advocating.And all this just days after Sir Keir Starmer's successor emphasised the need to end "factionalism" within the Labour Party.At lunchtime, Burnham will gather his cabinet for the first time, after its appointment yesterday became something of a nocturnal affair.At gone 10pm on Monday night, there was still a minibus full of ministerial wannabes inside Downing Street waiting for their new positions to be confirmed.One of the later posts to be filled was that of defence secretary, a role taken by Wes Streeting, whose resignation as health secretary two months ago contributed to the draining of Sir Keir's authority as prime minister.One of the key stories of the reshuffle was a pretty significant cull of the ministers closest too and most loyal to the now former prime minister, Darren Jones among them, but also David Lammy, Peter Kyle, Lord Hermer, Steve Reed and Rachel Reeves – albeit Reeves, I am told, was offered a cabinet post but turned it down.The question, in the coming days, weeks and months is how much do we hear in public from those banished Starmerites, as Andy Burnham's government sets out its stall.I hadn't anticipated it starting so soon.

schema.headline: Starmer ally takes swipe at Burnham's first big policy

title: CJP's parliament march from Jantar Mantar: India's 'cockroach' protest continues a day after police crackdown
description: Warning: This report contains images and description that some may find disturbingPolice and Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government have not commen
body: Warning: This report contains images and description that some may find disturbingPolice and Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government have not commented on the use of sticks and tear gas against protesters. Opposition parties have condemned the violence.The CJP is a satirical political movement inspired by remarks made earlier this year by India's chief justice, who used the word "cockroach" to describe unemployed young people turning to journalism and activism. After the comments sparked outrage, he said he had been referring only to those with "fake and bogus degrees".Monday's huge turnout showed that the CJP, though not a registered political party, has evolved into a movement capable of drawing tens of thousands onto the streets. Protesters, who have been camped for the past month at Jantar Mantar, a 300-year-old observatory in Delhi, gained global attention after activist and educationist Sonam Wangchuk joined them on 28 June and began an indefinite hunger strike in support of their demands.He was forcibly removed from the protest site by the police on Saturday and taken to hospital where he continues to fast.The movement has broadened into one of the most visible expressions of public dissent against Modi in recent years.Wangchuk and CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke have received support from most of the major opposition parties, farmer leaders, activist and celebrities, including Bollywood actors.Leader of the opposition Rahul Gandhi said said that the students' demands were "legitimate" and described Modi "as the most anti-youth prime minister India has ever had".The government did not engage with the demonstrators until Monday when Health Minister JP Nadda met some CJP leaders to hear their demands.The meeting took place against the backdrop of tens of thousands of people turning up from all over the country to participate in the CJP's call to march to parliament on the opening day of the monsoon session.Nadda, who is also a senior leader of the ruling BJP, said: "The meeting took place in a cordial atmosphere. I have requested all the protesters to end their sit-in and assist the administration in restoring normalcy."CJP spokesperson Saurav Das said the group's 10-minute meeting with Nadda ended with a promise that the government would look into the matter, but no concrete assurances. Another CJP leader, Ashutosh Ranka, dismissed the four-hour wait for the meeting as "a waste of time".At one point on Monday evening, it seemed like that the CJP might be willing to call off their protest following their meeting. But their stance appeared to harden after internet services - apparently blocked in the area throughout the day - were restored, revealing the scale of the violence.During the day, I saw protesters bring injured people to Jantar Mantar, including a young man bleeding heavily from a head wound as volunteer doctors bandaged him, and another student with a broken leg.Around the time the protesters were meeting Nadda, police and paramilitary forces moved in to clear Jantar Mantar. Among them were plainclothes officers, many with their faces covered, who joined uniformed police in chasing protesters and beating them with sticks.An announcement repeatedly made on a loudspeakers warned the protesters to "leave now, this protest is over". As protesters fled in panic, scrambling over police barricades to escape, I saw people fall to the ground, others struck with sticks, friends and siblings separated, and women in tears. The senior police officer leading the operation declined to answer questions. Minutes later, a senior female officer was sent to escort me out.Once internet access was restored, social media filled with videos showing security forces beating protesters, including women, with many lying unconscious on the ground.On Monday night, the CJP reclaimed the Jantar Mantar protest site and posted on social media, "This peaceful protest is not ending!" "Despite the police brutality, the brave protestors of CJP are back. Delhi, come out and join us at Jantar Mantar - this is now or never! Dharmendra Pradhan Must Resign!" the message said.In a statement, the police later said the protesters displayed "unruly, aggressive and violent behaviour", refused to disperse and violated prohibitory orders despite warnings."The protesters attacked police personnel with stones and other objects, attempted to breach police barricades, vandalised police and other government vehicles, damaged public property, and resorted to large-scale violence," it said.The CJP rejected the police account, accusing officers of being the aggressors. In social media posts, it urged injured supporters or those needing legal assistance to contact the group."I apologise to all our supporters, especially the girls who were brutally beaten by male police officers. We could have done better. I could have done better to protect you from the inhumane actions of the Delhi police," Dipke posted on X.In another post, he said one injured supporter was in critical condition in hospital and that he was going to visit her.The CJP is demanding an overhaul of India's education system, with greater accountability for alleged exam paper leaks. It made the resignation of Pradhan their main demand after the leak of the National Eligibility cum Entrance Test (NEET).Nearly 2.28 million candidates sat the medical entrance exam on 3 May. It was cancelled days later following reports that the paper had leaked, and a re-test was held weeks later. More than 20 students have since died by suicide, with their families saying they were devastated by the scandal.Protesters say Pradhan should take moral responsibility for the leak and resign. He has rejected the demand, calling the CJP and its supporters "the B-team of disruptive elements".But Monday's huge turnout, the violence and the CJP's refusal to leave the protest site are likely to keep the movement in the spotlight.

schema.headline: CJP's parliament march from Jantar Mantar: India's 'cockroach' protest continues a day after police crackdown

title: Louise Haigh: Labour power broker behind Burnham's rise
description: Louise Haigh had enjoyed a stratospheric political rise before her ministerial career came to a juddering halt shortly after Labour's return to power.
body: Louise Haigh had enjoyed a stratospheric political rise before her ministerial career came to a juddering halt shortly after Labour's return to power.She became the youngest-ever female cabinet minister at the age of 36, but resigned as transport secretary just four months into the role, after it emerged she had pleaded guilty to a fraud offence during her twenties.Now Andy Burnham has brought her back into government with a big job running the Cabinet Office and the title of first secretary of state, denoting her senior rank.It cements the Sheffield MP's place at the heart of Burnham's cabinet, having been among a tiny circle of key allies entrusted with holding "access talks" with civil servants ahead of his arrival in Downing Street.After her departure from the cabinet in November 2024, Haigh maintained a high profile on the backbenches, playing a key role in orchestrating the Labour rebellion against proposed welfare cuts last year.She went on to revive the Tribune group, a caucus dating back to the 1960s that has  emerged as a home for the "soft left" wing of the party that has fuelled Burnham's rise to power.Becoming increasingly critical of the Starmer government, she signed up to the Burnham project in the run-up to elections scheduled for May this year, reportedly after meeting him in Manchester to sound out his plans for power.She then oversaw his successful bid to return to Westminster as MP for Makerfield, living out of an AirBnb and running a campaign HQ in a community club shared with weddings, tribute bands and exotic bird auctions.She admits that she was initially sceptical about using a seat outside Wigan as the launch pad for his Westminster return, branding it a "huge risk". But it was a decision that has ultimately paid off for both of them, paving the way for his return to Parliament and her elevation back into the cabinet.Born and raised in Sheffield, Haigh has said party politics did not play a big role in her early life before she joined the Labour Party in her teens after being influenced by a "very left-wing economics teacher".Her parents separated in her early teens and she lived with her mother, who enrolled her in a private girls' school in the city, something she would later admit to finding "quite uncomfortable" as a Labour MP.She started a degree at the London School of Economics, but left after "absolutely hating" student life in the capital, instead deciding to study politics at Nottingham University.She went on to work for various Labour MPs, including party veteran Jon Trickett and Lisa Nandy, and became a volunteer police officer for the Met.As shop steward for parliamentary staff at Unite the union, she spearheaded a cost of living push to reduce the price of food and snacks on the parliamentary estate, and for a mothballed bar to be reopened by a "workers' co-operative".Elected in 2015 as MP for Sheffield Heeley at the age of 27, she nominated Jeremy Corbyn instead of Andy Burnham in that year's Labour leadership contest, a decision she would claim to regret in more recent years.She later recalled getting to know Burnham after her mother confronted him at a party hustings in Doncaster, to ask whether he planned to give her daughter a job if his bid for the Labour crown was successful.Corbyn gave Haigh her first shadow ministerial role, and kept her on his frontbench despite her backing Owen Smith's failed 2016 bid to topple him as Labour leader.Despite nominating former boss Nandy in the 2020 contest to replace him, eventual winner Sir Keir Starmer promoted her into the shadow cabinet as shadow Northern Ireland secretary, before she later became shadow transport secretary.It was in this role that she forged a close working relationship with England's metro mayors, including Burnham, who by that point had begun his second term as mayor of Greater Manchester.After Labour's election victory, she took the helm at the transport department, making headlines with an early decision to give train drivers a 14% pay rise across three years to end their long-running strike action.She was dubbed "Red Lou" in the press, reflecting her vibrant shades of dyed hair as well as her emerging reputation as one of the more left-leaning members of Starmer's cabinet.Her criticism of P&O Ferries' decision to sack hundreds of workers in 2022 led to a public rebuke by the prime minister, after the remarks threw the attendance of its parent company at a government investment summit into doubt.Just a month later came her resignation, after her previous conviction for making a false report to police in 2013 was revealed in the media.The incident dated back to her time working at insurance giant Aviva, when a work phone she had reported as stolen was later found. She said it was a "genuine mistake" and she had pleaded guilty on the advice of her lawyer.Haigh said she had told Sir Keir about the episode when Labour was in opposition, but was asked by Downing Street to offer her resignation after the story broke because "additional information" had come to light."But at no point would any of them tell me what that additional information was," she recently said in an interview.Her resignation, however, afforded her the freedom to expound the political ideas that are expected to feature heavily in Burnham's administration. In two essays published shortly before his Makerfield campaign, Haigh set out a vision for a radical reshaping of the British state.This included a significant loosening of Labour's debt rules, with the rolling five-year debt target extended instead over 10 years, and a call to "re-examine the mandate" of the Bank of England with a greater focus on growth.She also argued for several key policies previously advocated by the new prime minister, including a new tax on the value of land to replace stamp duty on house purchases."The status quo is not sustainable," she wrote. "We cannot afford to wait any longer before we change the system we inherited."It is a task she will now be charged with delivering, from a seat in the cockpit of the new Burnham administration.

schema.headline: Louise Haigh: Labour power broker behind Burnham's rise

title: The 20-somethings betting big on tech stocks
description: In her teens, Michelle Huynh, the eldest daughter of migrant parents in Australia, made a promise to her family that she would become a millionaire by
body: In her teens, Michelle Huynh, the eldest daughter of migrant parents in Australia, made a promise to her family that she would become a millionaire by the time she turned 30.The 26-year-old describes it as "a somewhat silly promise" inspired by the sacrifices her non-English-speaking parents made to raise the family.But she is trying to make that dream come true by investing her savings in the stock market."Times are so different and investing has become a necessity," says Huynh, who works in sales for a tech firm. "It feels like our purchasing power is shrinking. This is the only way to combat that."This year, the technology-driven surge in stock markets has edged her closer to that goal.With more than a third of her investments in tech stocks, by the middle of July that part of her savings had jumped this year by 50% - a rise of A$31,000 (£16,100; $21,666).But those gains have now eased to about A$22,000 as the sector is going through what she calls a "wild moment".Huynh says she's prepared for the volatility, viewing those investments as a long-term bet.The rise in tech stocks, led by firms riding the artificial intelligence (AI) boom, has attracted large numbers of ordinary investors, many of them in their 20s and early 30s, even as some analysts warn that the fervour around AI may be overblown.Retail investors have been caught up in the excitement, which has been fuelled by social media and marketing efforts to draw non-professionals, says Glenn Tan from advisory firm Providend.The tech-heavy Nasdaq in the US is up by about 10% this year, while Japan's Nikkei 225 has risen by more than 20%.But many tech stocks have seen big swings - both up and down.That volatility is most apparent in South Korea. Seoul's Kospi index, which includes tech heavyweights like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics, has jumped by more than 50% since January.The rally has attracted an army of retail investors, known locally as "ants", which has helped fuel volatile trading."I could maybe count with my hands the number of people who aren't investing today," South Korean investor U Chan Lee says. "Even stay-at-home mothers, like my mum, who has never been interested in the stock market, are now interested."But the Kospi has seen some dramatic moves. Since hitting a record high of more than 9,000 points in June it has plunged to around 6,500.Trading on the benchmark index has been halted seven times this year under a mechanism called the circuit breaker, designed to calm panic selling after it fell by 8%.Those slides have raised concerns over people who have borrowed money to invest in stocks, leading to South Korean authorities taking action to curb the practice.Lee, 30, sold many of his shares last year when the Kospi surged, concerned the market was becoming "too overheated". Since then, he has shifted to buying stocks when they fall and selling them when they rise a few days later.Singaporean business student Shyan Lim is a firm believer in AI-related shares, putting about three-quarters of his savings into tech stocks.The 24-year-old says there have been plenty of "uneasy" days when his investments plunged by as much as 10%, but he is willing to take his chances.Overall, his bets have worked so far. In October, he invested 23,000 Singapore dollars (£13,185; $17,845) in chipmakers Intel and Micron. Those shares are now worth about 100,000 Singapore dollars."It feels like I'm one step closer to retirement," Lim says. "While I'm still young I think I can take the risk. I probably won't take such positions when I'm older."With more than half of his investments in tech, George Lee, who recently graduated, says he is well aware of the lack of resources and other disadvantages that ordinary traders can face."Investing doesn't just come down to luck, though it does play a part," Lee says."As long as the fundamentals of a company don't change, I'm ready for the swings since I have time to let my shares run."

schema.headline: The 20-somethings betting big on tech stocks

title: Jeffrey Donaldson: Emma Little-Pengelly was 'floored' by news of former DUP leader's arrest
description: Northern Ireland's deputy first minster says she was "completely floored" when she first heard of the arrest and charges of former Democratic Unionist
body: Northern Ireland's deputy first minster says she was "completely floored" when she first heard of the arrest and charges of former Democratic Unionist Party (DUP) leader Jeffrey Donaldson over historical sex abuse.In June, he was convicted of 18 counts of sex abuse, including one count of rape, which he committed against two women when they were children.Emma Little-Pengelly said that people who worked alongside Donaldson over the past 20 years are "floored, shocked and utterly repulsed".On Friday, it emerged Donaldson is to appeal his conviction.After the former DUP leader was found guilty, a BBC Spotlight investigation reported on the double life of Donaldson.The programme heard from former senior police officers and politicians who spoke about behaviour they described as hypocritical and which put Donaldson in danger of being compromised by his political opponents.Speaking to the BBC's Red Lines podcast, Little-Pengelly said those reports should not take away from the scale of the crimes that he has been found guilty of."He's been convicted of being a paedophile and a rapist, and yes, there's all of these other allegations, but they absolutely pale into insignificance of that," she said."The focus should always be on the fact that he's been convicted of being a rapist and a paedophile, and that the victims of that have gone through a terrible ordeal to get justice."Former DUP MP Ian Paisley previously told the BBC he saw Donaldson "blindingly drunk" during a St Patrick's trip to Washington. However, Little-Pengelly said she did not see anything that made her think Donaldson was a hard drinker. "I can say hand in my heart, I didn't see anything that made me think that there were any issues in terms of problematic behaviour in regards to drinking or in terms of sexually inappropriate behaviour," she added."If any politician was running around acting in that way, I think it would have been the talk of St Patrick's in [Washington] DC amongst the many, many, people from Northern Ireland that go out there.”Little-Pengelly said she finds it "astounding that people haven't been talking about that for the last 20 years".She said Donaldson would have had a glass of wine at events but she never saw him drinking too much."On some of this other stuff, look, I don't know whether some of these allegations are true or they're not true. "All I can say is that I never saw problematic drinking from Jeffrey Donaldson. "If anything, Jeffrey Donaldson was somebody who seemed to work all of the time."The full BBC Red Lines interview with Emma Little-Pengelly is available now on BBC Sounds.

schema.headline: Jeffrey Donaldson: Emma Little-Pengelly was 'floored' by news of former DUP leader's arrest

title: AFC Bournemouth: Alex Scott rejects new contract with Premier League club
description: Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott has rejected a new contract with growing Premier League interest in the England Under-21 international.The Cherries
body: Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott has rejected a new contract with growing Premier League interest in the England Under-21 international.The Cherries have made it a summer priority to tie Scott down to fresh terms but as things stand there is no agreement.The development comes amid interest from Arsenal, Manchester United, Tottenham, Manchester City and Chelsea.BBC Sport reported in June that Arsenal saw an approach for Scott turned down by Bournemouth.The 22-year-old, who joined Bournemouth from Bristol City in 2023 for £25m, has two years left on his current deal with the Premier League side.Bournemouth remain steadfast in their position that Scott is not for sale, despite him rejecting the contract offer that had been on the table for at least two months.Bournemouth have insisted publicly, directly to the player and to interested clubs that he will remain on the south coast, even on his current contract.That stance has been consistent and, despite talk of an £80m valuation, they have continued to reject approaches.The south coast club are under no pressure to sell after qualifying for the Europa League and generating significant income through the sales last summer of Dean Huijsen, Milos Kerkez, Illia Zabarnyi and Dango Outtara.They have taken a similar stance with highly-rated forward Junior Kroupi, who is attracting interest from Arsenal and Tottenham. Brazil international Rayan is also not expected to leave and has given no indication that he wants to, having only joined the club in January.

schema.headline: AFC Bournemouth: Alex Scott rejects new contract with Premier League club

title: Key takeaways as Andy Burnham becomes PM
description: Image source, APByPaul SeddonPolitical reporterAndy Burnham has replaced Sir Keir Starmer as UK prime minister, after becoming Labour leader last week
body: Image source, APByPaul SeddonPolitical reporterAndy Burnham has replaced Sir Keir Starmer as UK prime minister, after becoming Labour leader last week.The former mayor of Greater Manchester becomes the country's fourth prime minister in five years and the seventh since 2016.Burnham says rough sleeping and the cost of living are prioritiesIn his first speech in Downing Street, Burnham announced he would "end rough sleeping in our country".This was swiftly followed by a No 10 press release promising an extra £340m for the task over the next five years from within the housing department budget.He also promised a new 10-year plan for Britain - to be unveiled later this year - and to build a new economy, putting "life's essentials back under public control".But arguing that people also needed "breathing space now", he said announcements on tackling the cost of living would start on Tuesday.Other changes he promised included changing the education system to help young people into work, building more council homes, and honouring commitments to fund defence while sticking to the UK's spending and debt rules.Cabinet picksBurnham made top appointments to his cabinet, making John Healey his chancellor in an move that took Westminster by surprise.Rachel Reeves had already confirmed she would be leaving the role and would not be serving in Burnham's cabinet, turning down the offer of another job.In other top moves, former energy secretary Ed Miliband has been promoted to foreign secretary, with Shabana Mahmood reappointed home secretary.Louise Haigh, a key ally who oversaw his preparations for No 10, has been given the job of running the Cabinet Office and appointed first secretary of state.Appointments, including to roles below the cabinet rank, are expected to continue on Tuesday. The new PM didn't use a lecternImage source, EPA/ShutterstockThere was much that was familiar about Burnham's speech in front of the famous black No 10 door, which was thronged with staffers and supporters. But perhaps conscious that "lectern moments" have become a symbol of the UK's political instability in recent years, Burnham broke with recent tradition and made the address without notes or a podium.He said that he was "acutely conscious" of the churn in the office behind him, stressing that the UK badly needed to regain stability.This would require politicians to "make politics work better" and "raise our game and rise to the new challenge", he added.Andy Burnham: Who is the new PM?From Manchester mayor to prime minister; what is his record and what does he stand for?Starmer and Burnham visited the KingKing Charles III said his farewells to Sir Keir in a final private audience at Buckingham Palace, before greeting Burnham.His role was central to the day because prime ministers are formally appointed by the sitting monarch, who is head of state.The short audience is known as "kissing hands" - although in practice, these days male premiers simply bow and shake hands with the monarch after accepting their offer to form a government.Image source, PA MediaNo hard feelings from StarmerEarlier, Sir Keir gave a short departure speech in Downing Street.With his supporters lining the street, he repeated key themes from the speech announcing his resignation in June, such as turning around the fortunes of the Labour Party and lowering child poverty.Despite leaving just two years after his landslide victory at the 2024 election, he insisted that he was proud of his record in office, saying Britain was "now stronger and fairer"."I go with good grace, I go with a smile," he said. He had time for a hug afterwards with Rachel Reeves, his chancellor and Downing Street neighbour during his time in office. Image source, EPA/ShutterstockGet in touchWhat questions do you have about the new PM and his policies?

schema.headline: Key takeaways as Andy Burnham becomes PM

title: Nations Championship: England's Ben Earl calls for brutal change after "brutal" schedule
description: Ben Earl has called for a change to the Nations Championship format after England's "brutal" schedule in the summer leg of the inaugural competition.E
body: Ben Earl has called for a change to the Nations Championship format after England's "brutal" schedule in the summer leg of the inaugural competition.England had to play three Tests in three different continents on successive weekends and accumulated 25,000 miles of travel.Victory over Argentina on Saturday keeps Steve Borthwick's team in the hunt for a place in the final on 29 November at Twickenham's Allianz Stadium.However, four long haul and four short haul flights in the past four weeks have contributed to players losing sleep and a reduced training schedule."It's been brutal, there's no two ways about it," England number 8 Earl said."We've been speaking about it – why would we not all be playing in one country? So you just don't travel, you go to one nation and have that festival feel."I'm not paid to make those decisions but it has been tough. It's been tough coming at the end of what's been quite a long season anyway for most of us."It's been back and forth, coming back to England, seeing your families and then going away again."It has seemed a bit weird but it's just something we're going to have to get used to because I think it's just the beginning."The competition resumes later in the year for the autumn leg with England next in action against Australia on 8 November.

schema.headline: Nations Championship: England's Ben Earl calls for brutal change after "brutal" schedule

title: World Matchplay Darts 2026 results: Luke Humphries suffers shock first-round loss to Cameron Menzies
description: World number two Luke Humphries is out of the World Matchplay after a shock first-round loss to Scotland's Cameron Menzies.The former world champion w
body: World number two Luke Humphries is out of the World Matchplay after a shock first-round loss to Scotland's Cameron Menzies.The former world champion was punished for some poor finishing and lost his first six legs with the darts before finally holding throw.Humphries threatened to stage a dramatic comeback, winning four legs on the spin to get back to 8-7 but Menzies sealed a stunning 10-7 victory in style by reeling in the 'big fish' - a maximum 170 checkout.The result will come as a huge disappointment to Humphries after he had shown signs of returning to top form - beating Luke Littler to win the US Darts Masters in June - in the past couple of months after a slow start to the year.But for Menzies, ranked 27th in the world, it is a momentous victory and one he says he "will take to his grave" as he becomes one of just three unseeded players to advance at this year's tournament.The Scot will now face 15th seed Ross Smith in the second round on Wednesday after the Englishman's 10-6 win over Netherlands' Kevin Doets.Both players averaged over 100 and managed over 50% on the doubles in a high-quality match but Smith hit when it mattered most to progress.Meanwhile, Gerwyn Price survived a scare before overcoming Germany's Martin Schindler 11-9.The Welshman admitted it was "probably one I should've lost" after falling 4-0 and 6-2 behind.Even after fighting back to take his first lead at 9-8, Price was made to work to finish it off as Schindler hit a 121 checkout to break back before the seventh seed eventually sealed the win in the 20th leg.It will be a battle of two former world champions in round two as Price faces Rob Cross after the Englishman's comprehensive 10-3 win over 10th seed Danny Noppert of the Netherlands.

schema.headline: World Matchplay Darts 2026 results: Luke Humphries suffers shock first-round loss to Cameron Menzies

title: Does Scotland have the helicopters it needs to fight wildfires?
description: Aerial firefighting pilots complete training far beyond standard helicopter flight. They're skilled in water collection, bucket deployment and precisi
body: Aerial firefighting pilots complete training far beyond standard helicopter flight. They're skilled in water collection, bucket deployment and precision flying.Skyhook's helicopters can be fitted out with "Bambi Buckets". These carry up to 1,000 litres of water and allow repeated pick-ups and water drops.But it says response times to wildfires will depend on any other job the helicopter is undertaking at the time. "As a commercial operator, we often receive requests several hours after a wildfire has already taken hold," a spokesperson tells the BBC."In many cases, an earlier aerial response has the potential to support ground crews while a fire is smaller and more easily contained."However, as a commercial helicopter operator, we are not currently funded or contracted by any organisation to maintain a dedicated aerial firefighting capability or crews on permanent standby."Skyhook, of course, does not want to see Scotland's landscape burn. But as a private operator, it must balance its wildfire response with its commitments to commercial clients."The Scottish government said it had learnt the lessons after Dava Moor," says Sandy McCook, from Nethy Bridge community council."Well I'm sorry, but the lessons have not been learnt. "It is untenable the position they are taking that they have enough equipment. It is helicopters that are needed."Andrew Baxter, Lib Dem MSP for Skye, Lochaber and Badenoch says he has taken the matter Scotland's Justice Secretary Neil Gray.Baxter says both the Scottish and UK governments should have "publicly available" firefighting aircraft during the wildfire season, rather than relying on private operators."At the moment it takes too long to get support to where it is needed," he says."We risk seeing serious loss of life unless we have a robust fire plan with aircraft that can be called upon at short notice."Neil Gray told the BBC that fire chiefs say they have access to "all the resources needed" to contain the Cairngorms blaze.But he said if there were "requests and innovations" over the growing threat of wildfires, he would discuss this with the SFRS.He added: "I'm incredibly proud of the response from officers."

schema.headline: Does Scotland have the helicopters it needs to fight wildfires?
